claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children you're responsible for, and if so, how many?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with me here in the UK. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information! It's helpful to know that you have Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with you in the UK.

To assess your eligibility properly, I need to ask: Are you currently responsible for Alex's care and upkeep (such as providing food, clothes, and accommodation)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm responsible for Alex's care - I provide their food, clothes, and accommodation. They live at home with me and I look after all their needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. Based on the information you've provided, I have a good understanding of your situation. Let me just ask one more question to ensure I have a complete picture:

Are there any special circumstances I should know about, such as:
- Is Alex in hospital or care?
- Does anyone else also claim to be responsible for Alex (such as another parent or guardian who might also want to claim)?
- Do you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000 (this wouldn't affect eligibility, but there may be a tax charge)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has actually been in hospital for a while now - about 3 months. But I'm still responsible for them and involved in their care. As for anyone else claiming, no, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that important information. I understand that Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months.

This is relevant because there are specific rules about Child Benefit when a child is in hospital. Let me ask you this:

Do you regularly spend money on Alex while they're in hospital (for example, on clothes, toys, treats, or other items for them)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I still buy things for Alex while they're in hospital - you know, their clothes, magazines, and their favorite food. I visit regularly and make sure they have what they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

**For Alex (aged 8):**
- Alex is under 16 years old
- Alex lives with you in the UK
- You are responsible for Alex's care and upkeep

Although Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (which is longer than the usual 12-week threshold), there's an important exception that applies to your situation: **you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in a UK hospital if you regularly spend money on them.** 

Since you've confirmed that you regularly purchase items for Alex (such as clothes, magazines, and food) while they're in hospital, this exception applies to you, and you remain eligible for Child Benefit.

You should make sure that HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) is aware of Alex's hospital stay, but based on your circumstances, your Child Benefit can continue.

If you haven't already claimed Child Benefit, you can proceed with your application. If you're already receiving it, you can continue to do so as long as you keep regularly spending money on Alex during their hospital stay.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK, and the claimant is responsible for their care and upkeep. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (which exceeds the 12-week threshold where Child Benefit payments might normally be affected), there is an exception to this rule: the claimant can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in a UK hospital if they regularly spend money on them. The claimant has confirmed they regularly purchase items for Alex including clothes, magazines, and food while visiting them in hospital. Therefore, this exception applies and the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 8), who lives with them in the UK. The claimant is responsible for Alex's care and upkeep. Although Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (exceeding the usual 12-week limit), the claimant continues to regularly spend money on Alex during their hospital stay. Under Child Benefit rules, this exception allows the claimant to continue receiving Child Benefit despite the extended hospital stay. No one else is claiming for Alex. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

The claimant has confirmed they regularly purchase items for Alex including clothes, magazines, and food while visiting them in hospital. Therefore, this exception applies and the claimant remains eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Although Alex has been in hospital for about 3 months (exceeding the usual 12-week limit), the claimant continues to regularly spend money on Alex during their hospital stay. Under Child Benefit rules, this exception allows the claimant to continue receiving Child Benefit despite the extended hospital stay. No one else is claiming for Alex. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
